questions.question: A patient with chest pain has ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF on a 12-lead ECG. Which coronary artery is the most likely culprit?
choices: Right coronary artery or left circumflex artery
questions.explanation: Leads II, III, and aVF are inferior leads, and ST elevation in these leads suggests an inferior STEMI. The culprit vessel is most often the right coronary artery, although the left circumflex artery can also be responsible. ST elevation in lead III greater than lead II often suggests right coronary artery occlusion and is frequently accompanied by reciprocal ST depression in leads I and aVL. With proximal right coronary artery occlusion, right ventricular infarction may coexist, so additional right-sided chest leads such as V4R should be considered.

questions.question: In a patient with chest pain suspicious for acute coronary syndrome, which finding is not one that should strongly suggest aortic dissection?
choices: Blood pressure difference or pulse difference between the right and left upper limbs
Chest pain with exertion that improves at rest
questions.explanation: Important clues to acute aortic dissection include sudden onset of severe chest or back pain, migrating pain, blood pressure or pulse differences between the arms, neurologic symptoms, syncope, and aortic regurgitation murmur. In contrast, chest pain that occurs with exertion and improves at rest typically suggests exertional myocardial ischemia such as stable angina, and is not a typical finding that should strongly suggest aortic dissection. Acute coronary syndrome and aortic dissection must be distinguished in patients with chest pain; when findings suggest dissection, evaluate with contrast CT or another appropriate test before giving antiplatelet agents, anticoagulants, or thrombolytic therapy.

questions.question: Which item is included in the Wells score for suspected lower-extremity deep vein thrombosis (DVT)?
choices: Paralysis, paresis, or recent plaster immobilization of the lower extremity
questions.explanation: The Wells score is used to estimate the clinical probability of DVT. Items include active cancer, paralysis or paresis or recent plaster immobilization of the lower extremity, recent bedridden status or major surgery, localized tenderness along the deep venous system, entire leg swelling, calf circumference difference, pitting edema, collateral superficial veins, and previous DVT. Hypotension, S1Q3T3, and hypoxemia may be relevant in pulmonary embolism assessment but are not items in the DVT Wells score.

questions.question: What is an appropriate initial approach for a hemodynamically stable patient with paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia (PSVT)?
choices: Attempt vagal maneuvers, including the modified Valsalva maneuver
Observe without giving a beta blocker
questions.explanation: In a hemodynamically stable regular narrow-QRS tachycardia suspicious for PSVT, first attempt vagal maneuvers such as the modified Valsalva maneuver. If ineffective, rapid IV adenosine may be considered after checking contraindications. If the patient is hemodynamically unstable with hypotension, shock, ischemic chest pain, acute heart failure, or impaired consciousness, synchronized cardioversion is indicated. Unsynchronized defibrillation is used for cardiac arrest rhythms such as VF or pulseless VT.

questions.question: Which ECG finding in hyperkalemia suggests severe disease?
choices: QT prolongation and prominent U waves
Short PR interval and delta wave
Loss of P waves, QRS widening, and a sine-wave pattern
Diffuse ST elevation and PR depression
questions.explanation: Hyperkalemia can progress from peaked T waves to PR prolongation, flattening or loss of P waves, QRS widening, and in severe cases a sine-wave pattern, ventricular fibrillation, or asystole. QRS widening or a sine-wave pattern suggests impending lethal arrhythmia and requires emergency treatment, including myocardial membrane stabilization with calcium. QT prolongation and prominent U waves suggest hypokalemia, a delta wave suggests WPW syndrome, and diffuse ST elevation with PR depression suggests acute pericarditis.

questions.question: In a patient with chest pain suspicious for acute coronary syndrome, ST elevation is observed in leads V1 to V4. Which coronary artery is the most likely culprit?
questions.explanation: ST elevation in leads V1 to V4 suggests an anterior-septal ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI), and the left anterior descending (LAD) artery is the most likely culprit vessel. Although left main trunk occlusion or proximal LAD lesions can cause extensive ischemia, an ST elevation pattern predominantly in V1-V4 points first to a LAD lesion.

questions.question: In a patient with chest pain suspicious for acute coronary syndrome, ST elevation is observed in leads I, aVL, V5, and V6. Which coronary artery is the most likely culprit?
questions.explanation: ST elevation in leads I, aVL, V5, and V6 suggests a lateral ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI). A diagonal branch can be the culprit in high lateral infarctions, while an obtuse marginal branch of the left circumflex (LCx) artery can be the culprit in lateral to posterolateral infarctions.

questions.question: Which ECG finding can occur in left main coronary artery occlusion or severe stenosis?
choices: ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF
ST elevation in leads V5 and V6
questions.explanation: Widespread subendocardial ischemia due to left main coronary artery disease, proximal LAD disease, or 3-vessel disease can present with ST elevation in lead aVR. Typically, this is accompanied by widespread ST depression in other leads. While ST elevation in lead aVR is not highly specific for left main disease, it is an important finding suggesting severe, life-threatening ischemia.

questions.question: Which coronary artery segment number corresponds to the left main trunk (LMT)?
questions.explanation: The left main trunk (#5) refers to the segment of the left coronary artery from its origin to its bifurcation into the left anterior descending (LAD) and left circumflex (LCx) arteries.

questions.question: Which coronary artery segment number corresponds to the proximal right coronary artery (RCA)?
questions.explanation: Segment #1 corresponds to the proximal right coronary artery (RCA). Occlusion of the proximal RCA can cause an inferior myocardial infarction, potentially complicated by right ventricular infarction or bradyarrhythmias due to AV node ischemia.

questions.question: Which coronary artery segment number corresponds to the proximal left anterior descending (LAD) artery?
questions.explanation: Segment #6 corresponds to the proximal left anterior descending (LAD) artery. Segment #7 refers to the mid LAD, #8 refers to the distal LAD or apex, and #9 refers to the first diagonal branch. A proximal LAD lesion is a critical lesion that can cause extensive anterior-septal wall ischemia.
